---
title: "Exam 1: Linear Algebra --- Answer Key"
subtitle: "Lessons 2--12 Comprehensive Assessment"
format:
  pdf:
    documentclass: article
    papersize: letter
    geometry:
      - margin=0.8in
    fontsize: 11pt
    colorlinks: true
    number-sections: false
    header-includes: |
      \usepackage{booktabs}
      \usepackage{amssymb}
      \usepackage{amsmath}
      \usepackage{mathtools}
      \usepackage{float}
      \usepackage{enumitem}
      \setlist{nosep, leftmargin=1.5em}
      \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.35}
---

\begin{tabular}{@{}ll@{}}
\toprule
\textbf{Time Allowed} & \~{}3 hours \\
\textbf{Total Points} & 100 \\
\textbf{Materials} & Pencil, paper, calculator (no CAS). No code. \\
\textbf{Format} & 10 questions, 10 pts each. Show all work. \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}

\vspace{0.8em}

> *Full worked solutions. Award partial credit for correct methodology even with arithmetic errors.*

\newpage

## Q1 (10 pts) --- Vectors, Bases, and Linear Combinations

**(a)** $\checkmark$ Two vectors in $\mathbb{R}^2$ are linearly independent iff neither is a scalar multiple of the other (equivalently, the $2 \times 2$ matrix they form has nonzero determinant).

$$\det\begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix} = (2)(-1) - (1)(1) = -3 \neq 0.$$

Since the determinant is nonzero, $\mathbf{b}_1$ and $\mathbf{b}_2$ are linearly independent. Two linearly independent vectors in $\mathbb{R}^2$ form a basis. $\square$

**(b)** We solve $c_1 \begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} + c_2 \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 5 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$, i.e.,

$$2c_1 + c_2 = 5, \qquad c_1 - c_2 = 1.$$

Adding: $3c_1 = 6 \Rightarrow c_1 = 2$. Then $c_2 = 5 - 2(2) = 1$.

$$\boxed{c_1 = 2, \quad c_2 = 1}$$

Check: $2\begin{pmatrix}2\\1\end{pmatrix} + 1\begin{pmatrix}1\\-1\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}4\\2\end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix}1\\-1\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}5\\1\end{pmatrix}$. $\checkmark$

**(c)** $\mathbf{w} = \begin{pmatrix}4\\2\end{pmatrix} = 2\begin{pmatrix}2\\1\end{pmatrix} = 2\mathbf{b}_1$.

Since $\mathbf{w}$ is a scalar multiple of $\mathbf{b}_1$, the set $\{\mathbf{b}_1, \mathbf{b}_2, \mathbf{w}\}$ is linearly **dependent**. (Also, any set of 3 vectors in $\mathbb{R}^2$ must be dependent since $\dim(\mathbb{R}^2) = 2$.)

**(d)** Since $\mathbf{w} = 2\mathbf{b}_1$, we have $\text{span}\{\mathbf{b}_1, \mathbf{w}\} = \text{span}\{\mathbf{b}_1\}$. This is the line through the origin in the direction $\begin{pmatrix}2\\1\end{pmatrix}$, which is a **one-dimensional subspace**, not all of $\mathbb{R}^2$.

**(e)** A basis for $\mathbb{R}^3$ requires exactly **3** linearly independent vectors (i.e., no vector can be written as a linear combination of the others). This number equals $\dim(\mathbb{R}^3) = 3$.

---

## Q2 (10 pts) --- Transformations, Basis Columns, and Matrix-Vector Products

**(a)** $A\mathbf{e}_1 = \begin{pmatrix}0\\1\end{pmatrix}$, $A\mathbf{e}_2 = \begin{pmatrix}-1\\0\end{pmatrix}$.

The standard basis vectors are rotated $90°$ counterclockwise. So $A$ is a **$90°$ counterclockwise rotation**.

**(b)** $B\mathbf{e}_1 = \begin{pmatrix}2\\0\end{pmatrix}$, $B\mathbf{e}_2 = \begin{pmatrix}0\\1\end{pmatrix}$.

$B$ **stretches** the $x$-direction by a factor of 2 and leaves the $y$-direction unchanged. The columns tell us exactly where the basis vectors land.

**(c)** Applying $A$ first then $B$ gives $C = BA$ (note: the matrix written on the left acts last):

$$C = BA = \begin{pmatrix}2 & 0\\0 & 1\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}0 & -1\\1 & 0\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}(2)(0)+(0)(1) & (2)(-1)+(0)(0)\\(0)(0)+(1)(1) & (0)(-1)+(1)(0)\end{pmatrix} = \boxed{\begin{pmatrix}0 & -2\\1 & 0\end{pmatrix}}$$

**(d)** Direct:
$$C\begin{pmatrix}3\\2\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}0 & -2\\1 & 0\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}3\\2\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}-4\\3\end{pmatrix}$$

Step-by-step verification:

$$A\begin{pmatrix}3\\2\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}0\cdot3 + (-1)\cdot2\\1\cdot3 + 0\cdot2\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}-2\\3\end{pmatrix}$$

$$B\begin{pmatrix}-2\\3\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}2(-2)\\1(3)\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}-4\\3\end{pmatrix} \quad\checkmark$$

**(e)**

$$AB = \begin{pmatrix}0 & -1\\1 & 0\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}2 & 0\\0 & 1\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}0 & -1\\2 & 0\end{pmatrix}$$

We already found $BA = \begin{pmatrix}0 & -2\\1 & 0\end{pmatrix}$.

Since $AB \neq BA$, **matrix multiplication is not commutative**. Geometrically, stretching then rotating gives a different result than rotating then stretching. The order of geometric transformations matters.

---

## Q3 (10 pts) --- Column Space, Null Space, and the Rank--Nullity Theorem

**(a)** Row-reduce $M$:

$$\begin{pmatrix}1 & 2 & 5\\2 & 1 & 4\\3 & 3 & 9\end{pmatrix} \xrightarrow{R_2 - 2R_1} \begin{pmatrix}1 & 2 & 5\\0 & -3 & -6\\3 & 3 & 9\end{pmatrix} \xrightarrow{R_3 - 3R_1} \begin{pmatrix}1 & 2 & 5\\0 & -3 & -6\\0 & -3 & -6\end{pmatrix} \xrightarrow{R_3 - R_2} \begin{pmatrix}1 & 2 & 5\\0 & -3 & -6\\0 & 0 & 0\end{pmatrix}$$

There are 2 pivots, so $\boxed{\text{rank}(M) = 2}$.

**(b)** The pivot columns are columns 1 and 2. A basis for $\text{Col}(M)$ is the corresponding columns of the **original** matrix:

$$\left\{ \begin{pmatrix}1\\2\\3\end{pmatrix},\; \begin{pmatrix}2\\1\\3\end{pmatrix} \right\}$$

**(c)** By the rank--nullity theorem: $\text{rank}(M) + \text{nullity}(M) = n$ (number of columns $= 3$).

$$\dim(\text{Null}(M)) = 3 - 2 = \boxed{1}$$

**(d)** From the echelon form, back-substitute. Let $x_3 = t$ (free variable).

Row 2: $-3x_2 - 6t = 0 \Rightarrow x_2 = -2t$.

Row 1: $x_1 + 2(-2t) + 5t = 0 \Rightarrow x_1 - 4t + 5t = 0 \Rightarrow x_1 = -t$.

$$\mathbf{x} = t\begin{pmatrix}-1\\-2\\1\end{pmatrix}$$

A basis for $\text{Null}(M)$ is $\left\{\begin{pmatrix}-1\\-2\\1\end{pmatrix}\right\}$.

Verification: $M\begin{pmatrix}-1\\-2\\1\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}-1-4+5\\-2-2+4\\-3-6+9\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}0\\0\\0\end{pmatrix}$. $\checkmark$

**(e)** A nonzero vector $\mathbf{x}$ in the null space means a nonzero production allocation $(-1, -2, 1)$ (scaled) yields zero total output. This means the third industry's output is completely expressible as a combination of the other two --- the industries have a **redundant dependency** among them. Essentially, one industry's production can be perfectly offset by the others.

---

## Q4 (10 pts) --- Determinants, Singularity, and Volume

**(a)**

$$\det(T) = (3)(2) - (1)(0) = \boxed{6}$$

The unit square's image has area $|\det(T)| = 6$.

**(b)** By the product rule: $\det(ST) = \det(S)\det(T)$.

$$\det(S) = (1)(3) - (-1)(0) = 3.$$

$$\det(ST) = 3 \times 6 = \boxed{18}$$

**(c)** $P$ is upper triangular, so $\det(P)$ is the product of diagonal entries:

$$\det(P) = (1)(1)(5) = \boxed{5}$$

For triangular matrices, row operations to eliminate off-diagonal entries produce no new terms --- the determinant is simply the product of pivots/diagonal entries.

**(d)** Row 2 of $Q$ is exactly $2 \times$ Row 1: $(2, 4, 6) = 2(1, 2, 3)$. Two proportional rows means the rows are linearly dependent, so $\det(Q) = 0$.

This means $Q$ is **singular** (not invertible). By rank--nullity: $\text{rank}(Q) \leq 2$, so $\dim(\text{Null}(Q)) \geq 1$. The transformation $Q$ collapses $\mathbb{R}^3$ into a lower-dimensional subspace.

**(e)**

- $\det(3R) = 3^3 \det(R) = 27 \times 7 = \boxed{189}$.

  Justification: for an $n \times n$ matrix, $\det(cR) = c^n \det(R)$ because scaling every row by $c$ multiplies the determinant by $c^n$.

- $\det(R^{-1}) = \dfrac{1}{\det(R)} = \boxed{\dfrac{1}{7}}$.

  Justification: $\det(RR^{-1}) = \det(I) = 1$, so $\det(R)\det(R^{-1}) = 1$.

---

## Q5 (10 pts) --- Eigenvalues, Diagonalisation, and Matrix Powers

**(a)** The characteristic equation:

$$\det(A - \lambda I) = \det\begin{pmatrix}3-\lambda & 1\\0 & 2-\lambda\end{pmatrix} = (3-\lambda)(2-\lambda) - 0 = 0$$

Since $A$ is upper triangular, the eigenvalues are the diagonal entries:

$$\boxed{\lambda_1 = 3, \quad \lambda_2 = 2}$$

**(b)** For $\lambda_1 = 3$: solve $(A - 3I)\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$:

$$\begin{pmatrix}0 & 1\\0 & -1\end{pmatrix}\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0} \Rightarrow v_2 = 0, \quad v_1 \text{ free.}$$

Eigenvector: $\mathbf{v}_1 = \begin{pmatrix}1\\0\end{pmatrix}$.

For $\lambda_2 = 2$: solve $(A - 2I)\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$:

$$\begin{pmatrix}1 & 1\\0 & 0\end{pmatrix}\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0} \Rightarrow v_1 + v_2 = 0 \Rightarrow v_1 = -v_2.$$

Eigenvector: $\mathbf{v}_2 = \begin{pmatrix}-1\\1\end{pmatrix}$.

**(c)**

$$P = \begin{pmatrix}1 & -1\\0 & 1\end{pmatrix}, \quad D = \begin{pmatrix}3 & 0\\0 & 2\end{pmatrix}$$

Verification (first column): $AP = A\begin{pmatrix}1\\0\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}3\\0\end{pmatrix} = 3\begin{pmatrix}1\\0\end{pmatrix}$, and $PD$ first column $= P\begin{pmatrix}3\\0\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}3\\0\end{pmatrix}$. $\checkmark$

Also: $P^{-1} = \begin{pmatrix}1 & 1\\0 & 1\end{pmatrix}$ (since $\det(P) = 1$).

**(d)**

$$A^{10} = PD^{10}P^{-1}, \quad D^{10} = \begin{pmatrix}3^{10} & 0\\0 & 2^{10}\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}59049 & 0\\0 & 1024\end{pmatrix}$$

**(e)**

$$A^{10}\mathbf{x}_0 = PD^{10}P^{-1}\begin{pmatrix}1\\1\end{pmatrix}$$

First: $P^{-1}\begin{pmatrix}1\\1\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}1 & 1\\0 & 1\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}1\\1\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}2\\1\end{pmatrix}$.

Then: $D^{10}\begin{pmatrix}2\\1\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}2 \cdot 59049\\1 \cdot 1024\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}118098\\1024\end{pmatrix}$.

Finally: $P\begin{pmatrix}118098\\1024\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}118098 - 1024\\1024\end{pmatrix} = \boxed{\begin{pmatrix}117074\\1024\end{pmatrix}}$.

The eigenvalue $\lambda_1 = 3$ **dominates** because $3^{10} \gg 2^{10}$. In the long run, the population vector aligns with the eigenvector $\begin{pmatrix}1\\0\end{pmatrix}$ (the direction associated with $\lambda_1 = 3$), meaning species 1 dominates while species 2 becomes proportionally negligible.

---

## Q6 (10 pts) --- SVD, Low-Rank Approximation, and Condition Number

**(a)** For $U$:

$$U^T U = \begin{pmatrix}1/\sqrt{2} & 1/\sqrt{2}\\1/\sqrt{2} & -1/\sqrt{2}\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}1/\sqrt{2} & 1/\sqrt{2}\\1/\sqrt{2} & -1/\sqrt{2}\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}1/2+1/2 & 1/2-1/2\\1/2-1/2 & 1/2+1/2\end{pmatrix} = I_2 \quad\checkmark$$

Similarly $V^T V = I_2$ by identical computation (the entries have the same structure). $\checkmark$

**(b)**

$$A = U\Sigma V^T = \begin{pmatrix}1/\sqrt{2} & 1/\sqrt{2}\\1/\sqrt{2} & -1/\sqrt{2}\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}6 & 0\\0 & 1\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}1/\sqrt{2} & 1/\sqrt{2}\\-1/\sqrt{2} & 1/\sqrt{2}\end{pmatrix}$$

Step 1: $U\Sigma = \begin{pmatrix}6/\sqrt{2} & 1/\sqrt{2}\\6/\sqrt{2} & -1/\sqrt{2}\end{pmatrix}$.

Step 2:

$$(U\Sigma)V^T = \begin{pmatrix}6/\sqrt{2} & 1/\sqrt{2}\\6/\sqrt{2} & -1/\sqrt{2}\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}1/\sqrt{2} & 1/\sqrt{2}\\-1/\sqrt{2} & 1/\sqrt{2}\end{pmatrix}$$

$$= \begin{pmatrix}6/2 - 1/2 & 6/2 + 1/2\\6/2 + 1/2 & 6/2 - 1/2\end{pmatrix} = \boxed{\begin{pmatrix}5/2 & 7/2\\7/2 & 5/2\end{pmatrix}}$$

**(c)** $\text{rank}(A) = 2$ (both singular values are nonzero). The singular values are $\sigma_1 = 6$ and $\sigma_2 = 1$.

**(d)** The best rank-1 approximation is:

$$A_1 = \sigma_1 \mathbf{u}_1 \mathbf{v}_1^T = 6 \begin{pmatrix}1/\sqrt{2}\\1/\sqrt{2}\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}1/\sqrt{2} & 1/\sqrt{2}\end{pmatrix} = 6\begin{pmatrix}1/2 & 1/2\\1/2 & 1/2\end{pmatrix} = \boxed{\begin{pmatrix}3 & 3\\3 & 3\end{pmatrix}}$$

**(e)** The condition number is:

$$\kappa(A) = \frac{\sigma_1}{\sigma_2} = \frac{6}{1} = \boxed{6}$$

If the input data has relative error $\epsilon = 10^{-3}$, the worst-case relative error in the solution is:

$$\kappa(A) \cdot \epsilon = 6 \times 10^{-3} = 6 \times 10^{-3}$$

So the output error can be amplified by up to a factor of 6 --- relatively mild conditioning.

---

## Q7 (10 pts) --- Dot Products, Projection, and Gram--Schmidt

**(a)**

$$\mathbf{a}_1 \cdot \mathbf{a}_2 = (1)(1) + (1)(-1) + (0)(1) = 1 - 1 + 0 = 0$$

Yes, the beams are **orthogonal**. $\checkmark$

**(b)**

$$\mathbf{f} \cdot \mathbf{a}_1 = (6)(1) + (3)(1) + (2)(0) = 9$$

$$\mathbf{a}_1 \cdot \mathbf{a}_1 = 1 + 1 + 0 = 2$$

$$\text{proj}_{\mathbf{a}_1}\mathbf{f} = \frac{9}{2}\begin{pmatrix}1\\1\\0\end{pmatrix} = \boxed{\begin{pmatrix}9/2\\9/2\\0\end{pmatrix}}$$

**(c)** Since $\mathbf{a}_1 \cdot \mathbf{a}_2 = 0$, the Gram--Schmidt step simplifies:

$$\mathbf{u}_2 = \mathbf{a}_2 - \frac{\mathbf{a}_2 \cdot \mathbf{u}_1}{\mathbf{u}_1 \cdot \mathbf{u}_1}\mathbf{u}_1 = \mathbf{a}_2 - \frac{0}{2}\mathbf{a}_1 = \mathbf{a}_2 = \begin{pmatrix}1\\-1\\1\end{pmatrix}$$

Since they were already orthogonal, $\mathbf{u}_2 = \mathbf{a}_2$. Verification: $\mathbf{u}_1 \cdot \mathbf{u}_2 = 0$. $\checkmark$

**(d)** Normalise:

$$\hat{\mathbf{u}}_1 = \frac{\mathbf{u}_1}{\|\mathbf{u}_1\|} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{pmatrix}1\\1\\0\end{pmatrix}, \quad \hat{\mathbf{u}}_2 = \frac{\mathbf{u}_2}{\|\mathbf{u}_2\|} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\begin{pmatrix}1\\-1\\1\end{pmatrix}$$

$$Q = \begin{pmatrix}1/\sqrt{2} & 1/\sqrt{3}\\1/\sqrt{2} & -1/\sqrt{3}\\0 & 1/\sqrt{3}\end{pmatrix}$$

$$Q^T Q = \begin{pmatrix}1/2+1/2+0 & 1/\sqrt{6}-1/\sqrt{6}+0\\1/\sqrt{6}-1/\sqrt{6}+0 & 1/3+1/3+1/3\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}1 & 0\\0 & 1\end{pmatrix} = I_2 \quad\checkmark$$

**(e)** The normal equations are $Q^T Q \hat{\mathbf{c}} = Q^T \mathbf{f}$. Since we just showed $Q^T Q = I_2$, this simplifies to:

$$\hat{\mathbf{c}} = Q^T \mathbf{f}$$

This works because when the columns of $Q$ are orthonormal, the projection onto each column is simply the dot product of $\mathbf{f}$ with that column. There is no "cross-talk" between the basis directions --- each coefficient is computed independently. Geometrically, orthonormal columns decompose the projection into mutually perpendicular components.

---

## Q8 (10 pts) --- Change of Basis, Norms, and Positive Definite Matrices

**(a)** $\det(P) = (1)(-1) - (1)(1) = -2$.

$$P^{-1} = \frac{1}{-2}\begin{pmatrix}-1 & -1\\-1 & 1\end{pmatrix} = \boxed{\begin{pmatrix}1/2 & 1/2\\1/2 & -1/2\end{pmatrix}}$$

**(b)**

$$SP = \begin{pmatrix}5 & 2\\2 & 2\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}1 & 1\\1 & -1\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}7 & 3\\4 & 0\end{pmatrix}$$

$$S_{\mathcal{B}} = P^{-1}(SP) = \begin{pmatrix}1/2 & 1/2\\1/2 & -1/2\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}7 & 3\\4 & 0\end{pmatrix} = \boxed{\begin{pmatrix}11/2 & 3/2\\3/2 & 3/2\end{pmatrix}}$$

**(c)** $S$ is symmetric since $S = S^T$: the off-diagonal entries are both 2. $\checkmark$

For positive definiteness, find the eigenvalues. The characteristic equation:

$$\det(S - \lambda I) = (5-\lambda)(2-\lambda) - 4 = \lambda^2 - 7\lambda + 6 = (\lambda - 6)(\lambda - 1) = 0$$

Eigenvalues: $\lambda_1 = 6 > 0$, $\lambda_2 = 1 > 0$.

Since $S$ is symmetric with all **positive eigenvalues**, it is **positive definite**. (Equivalently, for any nonzero $\mathbf{x}$, we can write $\mathbf{x} = c_1\mathbf{v}_1 + c_2\mathbf{v}_2$ in the eigenbasis, and $\mathbf{x}^T S \mathbf{x} = 6c_1^2 + c_2^2 > 0$.)

**(d)**

$$\|\mathbf{v}\|_1 = |3| + |-4| = 7$$

$$\|\mathbf{v}\|_2 = \sqrt{9 + 16} = \sqrt{25} = 5$$

$$\|\mathbf{v}\|_\infty = \max(|3|, |-4|) = 4$$

Ranking: $\|\mathbf{v}\|_\infty = 4 \leq \|\mathbf{v}\|_2 = 5 \leq \|\mathbf{v}\|_1 = 7$.

**(e)** Let $\mathbf{y} \neq \mathbf{0}$. We need to show $\mathbf{y}^T S_{\mathcal{B}} \mathbf{y} > 0$.

$$\mathbf{y}^T S_{\mathcal{B}} \mathbf{y} = \mathbf{y}^T (P^{-1} S P) \mathbf{y} = (P\mathbf{y})^T S^{-T} \cdot \ldots$$

More directly: define $\mathbf{z} = P\mathbf{y}$. Since $P$ is invertible and $\mathbf{y} \neq \mathbf{0}$, we have $\mathbf{z} \neq \mathbf{0}$.

Note that $S_{\mathcal{B}} = P^{-1}SP$ is a **similarity** transform. For the specific quadratic-form argument, observe that $S_{\mathcal{B}}$ is also symmetric (check: $S_{\mathcal{B}}^T = (P^{-1}SP)^T = P^T S^T (P^{-1})^T = P^T S (P^{-1})^T$, which equals $S_{\mathcal{B}}$ when $P$ is orthogonal, but more generally...).

The cleanest argument: $S_{\mathcal{B}}$ has the **same eigenvalues** as $S$ (similarity preserves eigenvalues). Since $S$ has eigenvalues 6 and 1, both positive, $S_{\mathcal{B}}$ also has all positive eigenvalues and is therefore positive definite. $\square$

---

## Q9 (10 pts) --- Least Squares, Projection, and the Normal Equations

**(a)**

$$A = \begin{pmatrix}1 & 0\\1 & 1\\1 & 2\end{pmatrix}, \quad \boldsymbol{\beta} = \begin{pmatrix}\beta_0\\\beta_1\end{pmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{T} = \begin{pmatrix}20\\17\\11\end{pmatrix}$$

**(b)** We have 3 equations in 2 unknowns. Checking: if $\beta_0 = 20, \beta_1 = -3$, then the predictions are $20, 17, 14$, but the third data point is 11, not 14. No line passes exactly through all three points, so $\mathbf{T} \notin \text{Col}(A)$.

$\text{rank}(A) = 2$ (the two columns are clearly independent: column 1 is all ones, column 2 is $0, 1, 2$).

**(c)**

$$A^T A = \begin{pmatrix}1 & 1 & 1\\0 & 1 & 2\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}1 & 0\\1 & 1\\1 & 2\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}3 & 3\\3 & 5\end{pmatrix}$$

$$A^T \mathbf{T} = \begin{pmatrix}1 & 1 & 1\\0 & 1 & 2\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}20\\17\\11\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}48\\39\end{pmatrix}$$

**(d)** Solve $\begin{pmatrix}3 & 3\\3 & 5\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}\hat{\beta}_0\\\hat{\beta}_1\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}48\\39\end{pmatrix}$:

From row 1: $3\hat{\beta}_0 + 3\hat{\beta}_1 = 48 \Rightarrow \hat{\beta}_0 + \hat{\beta}_1 = 16$.

From row 2: $3\hat{\beta}_0 + 5\hat{\beta}_1 = 39$.

Subtract 3 times row 1 equation from row 2: $2\hat{\beta}_1 = 39 - 48 = -9 \Rightarrow \hat{\beta}_1 = -9/2$.

Then $\hat{\beta}_0 = 16 - (-9/2) = 16 + 9/2 = 41/2$.

$$\boxed{\hat{\beta}_0 = \frac{41}{2} = 20.5, \quad \hat{\beta}_1 = -\frac{9}{2} = -4.5}$$

**(e)** The predicted values are:

$$A\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = \begin{pmatrix}1 & 0\\1 & 1\\1 & 2\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}20.5\\-4.5\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}20.5\\16\\11.5\end{pmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{T} - A\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = \begin{pmatrix}20\\17\\11\end{pmatrix} - \begin{pmatrix}20.5\\16\\11.5\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}-0.5\\1\\-0.5\end{pmatrix}$$

Check orthogonality:

$$A^T\mathbf{r} = \begin{pmatrix}1 & 1 & 1\\0 & 1 & 2\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}-0.5\\1\\-0.5\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}-0.5 + 1 - 0.5\\0 + 1 - 1\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}0\\0\end{pmatrix} \quad\checkmark$$

Geometrically, $A\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$ is the **orthogonal projection** of $\mathbf{T}$ onto $\text{Col}(A)$. The residual $\mathbf{r}$ is the component of $\mathbf{T}$ perpendicular to the column space. Since the least-squares solution minimises $\|\mathbf{r}\|^2$, the residual must be orthogonal to every vector in $\text{Col}(A)$ --- otherwise we could reduce $\|\mathbf{r}\|$ by adjusting the projection.

---

## Q10 (10 pts) --- The Unified SVD Picture

**(a)** For a diagonal matrix with positive entries, the SVD is trivial:

$$A = I \cdot \begin{pmatrix}4 & 0\\0 & 3\end{pmatrix} \cdot I^T$$

So $U = I$, $\Sigma = \begin{pmatrix}4 & 0\\0 & 3\end{pmatrix}$, $V = I$.

(The singular values must be listed in decreasing order: $\sigma_1 = 4 \geq \sigma_2 = 3$.)

**(b)** In the "rotate $\to$ scale $\to$ rotate" framework with $U = V = I$:

- **First rotation** ($V^T$): identity, so no rotation.
- **Scale** ($\Sigma$): stretch by 4 in the $x$-direction and by 3 in the $y$-direction.
- **Second rotation** ($U$): identity, so no rotation.

The unit circle $x^2 + y^2 = 1$ maps to the **ellipse** $\dfrac{x^2}{16} + \dfrac{y^2}{9} = 1$ with semi-axes $a = 4$ (horizontal) and $b = 3$ (vertical).

**(c)**

$$A^T A = \begin{pmatrix}4 & 0\\0 & 3\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}4 & 0\\0 & 3\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}16 & 0\\0 & 9\end{pmatrix}$$

Eigenvalues of $A^T A$: $\lambda_1 = 16$, $\lambda_2 = 9$.

These are the **squares of the singular values**: $\sigma_i = \sqrt{\lambda_i}$, so $\sigma_1 = 4, \sigma_2 = 3$.

**(d)**

$$\det(A) = (4)(3) = 12$$

The singular values multiply to give: $\sigma_1 \sigma_2 = 4 \times 3 = 12 = |\det(A)|$.

**General relationship:** For any $n \times n$ matrix $A$, $|\det(A)| = \prod_{i=1}^n \sigma_i$. The determinant measures the signed volume scaling factor, while the product of singular values measures the unsigned volume scaling factor.

**(e)** Let $B = RA$ where $R$ is a rotation.

- **Singular values of $B$**: The singular values of $B$ are the **same** as those of $A$: $\sigma_1 = 4$, $\sigma_2 = 3$. Reason: $B^T B = (RA)^T(RA) = A^T R^T R A = A^T A$ (since $R^T R = I$ for rotation matrices). The singular values depend only on $A^T A$.

- **$\det(B)$**: $\det(B) = \det(R)\det(A) = (1)(12) = 12$, since a rotation matrix has determinant 1.

- **$\text{rank}(B)$**: $\text{rank}(B) = 2$, since $R$ is invertible and multiplying by an invertible matrix preserves rank.

- **Eigenvalues of $B^T B$**: $B^T B = A^T A = \begin{pmatrix}16 & 0\\0 & 9\end{pmatrix}$, so the eigenvalues are $16$ and $9$.

The SVD interpretation: if $A = U_A \Sigma V_A^T$, then $B = R U_A \Sigma V_A^T$. Since $RU_A$ is also orthogonal, this is a valid SVD $B = U_B \Sigma V_B^T$ with the same $\Sigma$ and $V$. Rotation only changes the "output rotation" $U$, not the scaling or the "input rotation."

---

\vspace{1em}
\begin{center}
\textbf{--- End of Answer Key ---}
\end{center}
